questions.question:
An array of n numbers is given, where n is an even number. The maximum as well as the minimum of these n numbers needs to be determined. Which of the following is TRUE about the number of comparisons needed?
A. At least 2n - c comparisons, for some constant c, are needed.
B. At most 1.5n - 2 comparisons are needed.
C. At least 2n_0 comparisons are needed.
D. None of the above.
questions.answer: B. At most 1.5n - 2 comparisons are needed.
questions.to_find: The true statement about the minimum number of comparisons needed to find both max and min in an array of n even numbers.

understand.plain: We have an array of n numbers (n is even) and want to find both the maximum and minimum. The naive approach compares each element against current max and min, taking about 2(n-1) comparisons. However, a smarter 'tournament' approach pairs elements first and can do it in fewer comparisons. We need to identify which statement is true.

keywords.explain: Pair up elements, compare within each pair (n/2 comparisons), then find max among pair-winners (n/2 - 1) and min among pair-losers (n/2 - 1). Total = n/2 + n/2 - 1 + n/2 - 1 = 3n/2 - 2 comparisons.
keywords.example: For n=4: pair (a,b) and (c,d), say b>a and d>c. Then max = max(b,d) and min = min(a,c). Total = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 = 3*4/2 - 2 = 4. Correct.

keywords.explain: Any algorithm to find both max and min in an n-element array needs at least ceil(3n/2) - 2 comparisons. The tournament method achieves this optimally.
keywords.example: For n=6: lower bound = 3*6/2 - 2 = 7 comparisons needed.

understand.visual_alt: Tournament method pairing n=6 elements into pairs, making 3 pair-comparisons, then 2 comparisons among winners for max, 2 among losers for min. Total = 3n/2 - 2 = 7.

given.aim: Determine the true statement about comparisons needed to find both max and min in an even-length array of n numbers.

terms.meaning: Number of elements in the array, where n is even.
terms.connects: Determines the scale of the problem and bounds the comparison count.

terms.meaning: Compare elements in pairs first, then track max among winners and min among losers.
terms.connects: Achieves 3n/2 - 2 comparisons, which is the optimal lower bound.

terms.meaning: The optimal number of comparisons for simultaneous max-min finding: n/2 pair comparisons + (n/2-1) for max among winners + (n/2-1) for min among losers.
terms.connects: This equals 1.5n - 2, making option B correct.

given.plan: The optimal algorithm pairs elements, makes n/2 comparisons, then finds max among the larger elements (n/2-1 comparisons) and min among the smaller elements (n/2-1 comparisons). Total = n/2 + (n/2-1) + (n/2-1) = 3n/2 - 2 = 1.5n - 2. Check each option against this known optimal.

steps.title: Compute optimal comparison count using tournament method
steps.apply: Step 1: Pair up all n elements into n/2 pairs. Compare within each pair: n/2 comparisons. Now we have n/2 'larger' elements (candidates for max) and n/2 'smaller' elements (candidates for min). Step 2: Find max among n/2 larger elements: n/2 - 1 comparisons. Step 3: Find min among n/2 smaller elements: n/2 - 1 comparisons. Total = n/2 + (n/2-1) + (n/2-1) = 3n/2 - 2 = 1.5n - 2.
steps.note: For n=6: 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 = 1.5*6 - 2. This is also the known information-theoretic lower bound.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. 'At least 2n-c for some constant c': The algorithm uses 1.5n-2 which is less than 2n-c for large n. So this says we NEED at least 2n-c, but we can do it in 1.5n-2. FALSE (we don't need that many). B. 'At most 1.5n-2': We showed an algorithm doing exactly 1.5n-2 comparisons. TRUE. C. 'At least 2n_0 comparisons': FALSE since 1.5n-2 < 2n. D. None of the above: Incorrect since B is true.
steps.note: Option B is correct because 3n/2 - 2 is achievable (upper bound) and is the exact optimal (also a lower bound).

solution.result: B. At most 1.5n - 2 comparisons are needed.

questions.formula_note: Simultaneous max-min finding via tournament (pairing) method achieves the optimal 3n/2 - 2 comparisons, which equals 1.5n - 2.

questions.answer: B. T(n) = O(sqrt(n)) and T(n) = Omega(1)
questions.to_find: The correct asymptotic bounds (big-O and big-Omega) for T(n), the number of loop iterations in the IsPrime function.

understand.plain: The IsPrime function checks divisibility from i=2 up to sqrt(n). If a divisor is found, it exits early. T(n) is the number of loop iterations. The key insight is that the loop may exit early (when n is composite) or run all the way to sqrt(n) (when n is prime). We need to capture both best-case (Omega) and worst-case (O) behavior.

keywords.explain: O(f(n)) means the function grows no faster than f(n) in the worst case. Here worst case is n prime: loop runs floor(sqrt(n)) - 1 times, so O(sqrt(n)).
keywords.example: For n=7 (prime), loop runs i=2,3 (since sqrt(7)~2.64), T(7)=2. For n=100, worst case is prime near 100.

keywords.explain: Omega(f(n)) means there exists a family of inputs where the function grows at least as fast as f(n). If n is even (like n=4), the loop exits after i=2, so T(n)=1 regardless of how large n is. Hence T(n) = Omega(1).
keywords.example: For n=4: i=2, 4%2==0, exit. T(4)=1. For n=1000000 (even), T(n)=1 still.

understand.visual_alt: The IsPrime loop runs at most sqrt(n) times (when n is prime) and as few as 1 time (when n is even >2). So T(n) is O(sqrt(n)) in worst case and Omega(1) in best case.

given.aim: Find correct asymptotic worst-case (O) and best-case (Omega) bounds for loop iterations in IsPrime(n).

terms.meaning: Number of times the for loop body executes on input n.
terms.example: T(7)=2 (i=2,3 before loop ends since sqrt(7)<3), T(4)=1 (exits at i=2).
terms.connects: We need both an upper bound O and lower bound Omega for T(n).

terms.meaning: Happens when n is prime: the loop runs all the way from i=2 to i=floor(sqrt(n)), giving approximately sqrt(n) iterations.
terms.example: For n=101 (prime): loop runs i=2,3,...,10 (since sqrt(101)~10.05), so T(101)=9.

terms.meaning: Happens when n is even (e.g., n=4,6,8,...): the loop exits immediately at i=2 with just 1 iteration.
terms.example: T(2^k) = 1 for any k>=2, regardless of how large k is.
terms.connects: Establishes T(n) = Omega(1) — there exist infinitely many n for which T(n)=1.

given.plan: Determine the worst-case (when the loop runs the longest, i.e., n prime) to get the O bound, and the best-case (when the loop exits earliest, i.e., n even) to get the Omega bound. Match with options.

steps.title: Determine worst-case upper bound O(sqrt(n))
steps.apply: In the worst case, n is prime. The loop condition i <= sqrt(n) means i runs from 2 to floor(sqrt(n)). Number of iterations = floor(sqrt(n)) - 1, which is Theta(sqrt(n)). Therefore T(n) = O(sqrt(n)).
steps.note: The loop can never exceed sqrt(n) iterations regardless of n, so O(sqrt(n)) is a valid upper bound.

steps.title: Determine best-case lower bound Omega(1)
steps.formula_raw: T(n) >= 1 for all n >= 4; T(even n) = 1
steps.apply: Consider n = 2k for k >= 2 (even numbers). At i=2: n%2 == 0, so the loop exits with return 0 after exactly 1 iteration. T(2k) = 1 for all k >= 2. Since T(n) can be constant (=1) for infinitely many n, the lower bound is Omega(1), NOT Omega(sqrt(n)). Option A claims T(n) = Omega(sqrt(n)) which would mean T(n) >= c*sqrt(n) for all large n — false since T(even)=1.
steps.note: Omega(1) simply means 'at least a constant', which is trivially true. But it cannot be tightened to Omega(sqrt(n)) because even numbers give T=1.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: A. T(n)=O(sqrt(n)) and T(n)=Omega(sqrt(n)): FALSE — Omega(sqrt(n)) fails for even n. B. T(n)=O(sqrt(n)) and T(n)=Omega(1): TRUE — O(sqrt(n)) from prime case, Omega(1) from even case. C. T(n)=O(n) and T(n)=Omega(sqrt(n)): FALSE — Omega(sqrt(n)) part fails. D. None: FALSE since B is correct.
steps.note: Note: O(sqrt(n)) and O(n) are both technically true upper bounds, but O(sqrt(n)) is tighter. The question is about which complete statement is TRUE.

solution.result: B. T(n) = O(sqrt(n)) and T(n) = Omega(1)

questions.formula_note: Loop exit on finding a factor gives best-case Omega(1); running all iterations for primes gives worst-case O(sqrt(n)). The loop bound is governed by the trial division primality test.

questions.question:
The minimum number of comparisons required to determine if an integer appears more than n/2 times in a sorted array of n integers is
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(log n)
C. Theta(log^2 n)
D. Theta(1)
questions.to_find: The minimum number of comparisons needed to check if any integer appears more than n/2 times in a sorted array of n integers.

understand.plain: We have a sorted array of n integers. We want to know if any element appears more than n/2 times (i.e., is a majority element). In a sorted array, if a majority element exists, it must occupy the middle position (index n/2). So we just need to find the first and last occurrence of arr[n/2] using binary search, which takes O(log n) comparisons.

keywords.explain: An element appearing more than n/2 times. In a sorted array, if it exists, it must span the middle index.
keywords.example: Array [1,2,2,2,3]: n=5, n/2=2.5. Element 2 appears 3 times > 2.5. arr[2]=2 is the middle.

keywords.explain: Searching in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space, taking O(log n) comparisons.
keywords.example: Find first occurrence of x in sorted array: compare x with arr[mid], go left if equal, take O(log n).

Majority!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Sorted array [1,1,3,3,3,3,3,5] of n=8. Middle element arr[4]=3. Binary search finds first occurrence at index 2, last at index 6. Count=5 > n/2=4, so 3 is a majority element.

given.aim: Find the minimum comparisons to detect if any element appears more than n/2 times in a sorted array of n integers.

terms.meaning: Elements are arranged in non-decreasing order, enabling binary search.
terms.connects: Sorting allows us to use binary search instead of linear scan.

terms.meaning: An element appearing more than n/2 times is called a majority element.
terms.connects: In a sorted array, a majority element must occupy position floor(n/2) (the median position).

terms.term: First and last occurrence via binary search
terms.meaning: Using binary search, we can find the first and last index where arr[n/2] appears in O(log n) comparisons each.
terms.example: In [1,2,2,2,3]: first occurrence of 2 at index 1, last at index 3, count=3.
terms.connects: count = last - first + 1 > n/2 means majority element found. Total cost: O(log n).

given.plan: In a sorted array, if a majority element exists it must be arr[n/2]. Use binary search (O(log n) each) to find the first and last occurrence of arr[n/2]. Check if count > n/2. Total comparisons = Theta(log n).

steps.title: Key observation: majority element must be at middle index
steps.formula_raw: If majority exists, it occupies arr[floor(n/2)]
steps.apply: In a sorted array of n elements, if any element x appears more than n/2 times, then x must appear at position floor(n/2) (0-indexed). This is because all copies of x are contiguous in the sorted array and they span more than half the array, so the middle position must be x. Therefore, we only need to check arr[n/2] as the candidate.
steps.note: This reduces an arbitrary search to a single candidate check — no need to scan all elements.

steps.title: Count occurrences of arr[n/2] using binary search
steps.apply: Use binary search to find first_index: leftmost position where value equals arr[n/2]. Cost: O(log n). Use binary search to find last_index: rightmost position where value equals arr[n/2]. Cost: O(log n). Total count = last_index - first_index + 1. If count > n/2, arr[n/2] is a majority element. Total comparisons = O(log n) + O(log n) = Theta(log n).
steps.note: We cannot do better than Theta(log n) in the worst case because we need at least log n comparisons to locate any specific element in a sorted array.

steps.title: Verify the lower bound
steps.apply: A lower bound argument: we need to determine the first and last occurrence of the candidate, essentially two predecessor/successor queries on a sorted array. Any comparison-based algorithm for such queries needs Omega(log n) comparisons. So the answer is Theta(log n).
steps.note: Options: A. Theta(n) — too slow; B. Theta(log n) — correct; C. Theta(log^2 n) — too slow; D. Theta(1) — impossible.

questions.formula_note: Majority element in sorted array must lie at arr[n/2]; two binary searches to find first and last occurrence yield Theta(log n) total comparisons.

questions.question:
We have a binary heap on n elements and wish to insert n more elements (not necessarily one after another) into this heap. The total time required for this is
A. Theta(log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. Theta(n^2)
questions.to_find: The total time to insert n new elements into an existing binary heap of n elements.

understand.plain: We have a binary heap with n elements. We want to insert n more elements into it. One approach is to insert each element one by one (each taking O(log(current size)) time). But a smarter approach is to merge all 2n elements and build a new heap using the linear-time heapify (build-heap) algorithm. This takes Theta(n) total time.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property (parent >= children for max-heap). Supports insert in O(log n) and build from array in O(n).
keywords.example: A max-heap: root is 10, children 8 and 9, etc.

keywords.explain: Given an unsorted array of m elements, we can build a valid heap in O(m) time by calling sift-down on each non-leaf node from bottom up.
keywords.example: Array [3,1,6,5,2,4]: build-heap takes O(6) = O(n) time.

understand.visual_alt: Merging the existing n elements with n new elements gives 2n elements total. Applying build-heap on 2n elements takes O(2n) = Theta(n) time, which is faster than inserting one by one.

given.aim: Find the total time complexity to insert n new elements into an existing n-element binary heap.

terms.meaning: A valid binary heap structure with n elements already in heap order.
terms.connects: We already have these n elements correctly arranged.

terms.meaning: n additional elements to be added to the heap (not necessarily one at a time).
terms.example: 10 new values to add to the existing 10-element heap.
terms.connects: The key insight: 'not necessarily one after another' allows batch insertion via build-heap.

terms.meaning: Combine existing n elements and n new elements into an array of 2n elements, then apply build-heap in O(2n) = Theta(n) time.
terms.example: Merge [4,7,2,...] (heap) with [3,9,1,...] (new) into a 2n array, apply heapify.
terms.connects: This is the optimal strategy, making total time Theta(n).

given.plan: Since elements need not be inserted one at a time, we can use the build-heap algorithm on all 2n elements combined (the original n + n new elements). Build-heap runs in O(m) for m elements, so for 2n elements it is Theta(n). This is the optimal approach.

steps.title: Naive approach: insert one-by-one
steps.formula_raw: sum_{k=n+1}^{2n} O(log k) = O(n log n)
steps.apply: Inserting n elements one by one, each into a heap of growing size (n+1, n+2, ..., 2n), costs O(log(n+1)) + O(log(n+2)) + ... + O(log(2n)) = O(n log n). This is an upper bound but not optimal.
steps.note: The problem says 'not necessarily one after another', hinting we should use a smarter approach.

steps.title: Optimal approach: batch insert via build-heap
steps.apply: Combine the existing n heap elements and n new elements into an array of 2n elements. Apply the standard build-heap algorithm (start sift-down from last non-leaf to root). The build-heap algorithm runs in O(2n) = Theta(n) time. This is better than one-by-one insertion.
steps.note: Build-heap is O(n) because the sum of heights of all nodes in a complete binary tree of n nodes is O(n): sum_{h=0}^{log n} (n/2^(h+1)) * h = O(n).

steps.title: Verify lower bound
steps.formula_raw: Omega(n) since we must at least look at all n new elements
steps.apply: We must process all n new elements, so any algorithm needs at least Omega(n) time. Combined with the O(n) build-heap approach, the answer is Theta(n).
steps.note: This eliminates Theta(log n) (option A) as insufficient time. Options C and D are valid upper bounds but not tight.

questions.formula_note: Build-heap (linear-time heapify) algorithm on m elements runs in O(m). Merging n existing + n new elements and applying build-heap on 2n elements gives total time Theta(n).
